feedback.text: This project provides an opportunity for the European institutions to be able to design a Digital Fairness Act to effectively regulate current issues in relation to relations between consumers and suppliers of digital products. Against this background, it is proposed that future legislation incorporates the following key points: (I) The regulatory requirement provided for in the legislation to be developed should be adapted to the specific context of e-commerce, taking into account both the nature of the business and the profile of the consumer to whom the product is addressed. In this respect, it is inappropriate to apply a uniform regulatory framework to all operators, as not all sectors have the same characteristics or risks. Thus, where the target public includes particularly vulnerable groups, such as minors, a more stringent standard of compliance with the legal obligations contained in the future Digital Fairness Act (DFA) should be imposed. On the contrary, in cases where the product offered is not particularly complex (for example, ordinary consumer goods, such as a parking ticket), or it is addressed exclusively to older consumers who are not considered vulnerable, a lower intensity of the obligations required may be justified. Such differentiation is essential to ensure adequate consumer protection without compromising the operational viability of different business models in the digital environment. (ii) Therefore, it is proposed that the Digital Fairness Act includes proportionate clauses of transparency and information obligations in recurring digital transactions. Given that there are services that are characterised by low contractual complexity and high repeatability, such as non-essential services, the legislation should provide for simplified compliance models, provided that clarity, accessibility and respect for consumer rights are ensured. This simplification would help reduce unnecessary administrative burdens, improve user experience, facilitate their digital procurement, and foster the competitiveness of operators in the digital domain. (iii) It is also suggested that the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) includes harmonised criteria for the presentation of prices and promotions, allowing operators to adapt their business strategies without incurring the risk of non-compliance due to divergent interpretations between Member States. Such regulatory harmonisation would foster legal certainty by increasing the competitiveness of economic operators in the context of digital trade. Attention was paid to the concerns and comments raised.
